articles.title: Three ways people respond to a problem (other than solving it)
articles.summary: People often respond to problems by pushing them around, preserving them, or promoting new ones, in addition to solving them. These alternative approaches can be seen as a form of "P's" - Pushing problems around Preserving problems Promoting new problems. While some may view these responses as negative, they can also indicate underlying issues with the problem itself or the system that is trying to solve it.

articles.title: A Road to Lisp: Which Lisp
articles.summary: Lisp is a family of programming languages with many variations to choose from due to their shared fundamental syntax but differing operators, semantics, standard libraries, and capabilities. Common Lisp, also known as CL, is considered the most mature and comprehensive dialect, offering a broad set of features including functions for controlling compilation and evaluation from within the language itself. As a beginner, learning one dialect can be beneficial as it allows you to learn new concepts with any implementation, making it relatively easy to switch later on.

articles.title: PACT: Anonymous Credentials for the Web – Mozilla Hacks
articles.summary: PACT: Anonymous Credentials for the Web is a technical solution proposed by Mozilla that aims to address the issue of bots on the web, but its effectiveness in solving this problem has been questioned due to advances in generative AI rendering CAPTCHAs ineffective and browser privacy protections dismantling passive signals used to identify anti-abuse systems. Many sites are now opting for more invasive mechanisms requiring users to disclose identifying information or disable their VPN, leading to greater friction for users while compromising their privacy. This tension between protecting user privacy and preventing volumetric abuse has become increasingly acute, with website operators struggling to balance these competing interests.

articles.title: EEG shows brain can simultaneous encode two speech streams
articles.summary: Researchers have discovered that the brain simultaneously encodes two competing speech streams, a finding that could revolutionize our understanding of auditory attention and language processing. Using electroencephalography to record brain activity in normal-hearing adults, the study measured neural responses to background babble and simultaneous switching between two speech streams. The results show that the brain rapidly switches attention between the two streams, with the new target emerging before disengaging from the previous one. This finding suggests a transient simultaneous encoding of two speech streams, which could inform on cognitive effort during different phases of attention switch.

articles.title: The Zilog Z80 has turned 50
articles.summary: The Zilog Z80 processor has turned 50 years old, marking its official launch in July 1976. This processor was extremely successful, being used in many early personal computers, home and hobby computers, as well as embedded industrial applications. Its widespread use contributed to a de facto hardware standard for 8-bit micros, allowing a de facto software standard of CP/M and Microsoft BASIC. The Z80 itself spawned numerous clones and derived architectures over the years, including the Sharp LR35902 used in the original GameBoy.

articles.title: README, not
articles.summary: The concept of README files has been gaining traction in the programming community as a way to provide users with clear and concise information about how to use or interact with a project. A READMENOT file would serve as an unambiguous warning that the code within the project is unsuitable for direct human consumption, serving as a default explanation for why it shouldn't be read. This idea aligns with the debate surrounding what constitutes "slop" in programming, which can range from poorly developed code to lack of operator understanding. The proposed READMENOT file would provide a consistent marker for such projects, allowing users to make informed decisions about their interaction with them.
